narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 40% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to flag concerns about your readiness. Your profile shows strong alignment with many typical applicants—you're a white female planning full-time studies, with middle family income and an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, just slightly below the average of 3.21. However, your academic rankings stand out: in Year 1, you ranked in the 1st decile (bottom 10%) compared to the typical 6th decile, and in Year 3, you were in the 2nd decile versus the average 6th. Your LSAT score of 29 is also notably lower than the dataset average of about 37. When the model evaluated you, your Year 3 decile rank had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure because it's so much lower than what's typical for passers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which similarly pulled the odds downward due to its below-average level. Your Year 1 decile rank came third, reinforcing that early struggles carried weight. Family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, full-time status, and gender followed in descending order of impact, each nudging slightly toward the failure prediction but far less than your grades and test score. Overall, these academic signals dominated, painting a picture riskier than most applicants who pass the bar. The good news is you can take action: retake the LSAT to boost that score, as it's changeable and highly influential. Focus on strategies to improve your class rankings in future coursework, perhaps through tutoring or study habits, since those deciles mattered most. Building a stronger academic track record now could shift future predictions in your favor and open doors to law school. Keep pushing—you have time to turn this around.
